## [18:06 - 21:07]

I realize that they're not an opportunity at revenue. So a lot of people, what they do is they think that a lead is automatically an opportunity at revenue. It's not. 99% of deals are closed. There's four things that you need to understand. Number one, do they have the money? Do they have the budget? If you're in New York, San Francisco, et cetera. So here's what happens. VPs of sales, they're very quantitative. They're numbers people. ## [39:16 - 42:16]

So what I do is there's three steps on how I handle it. Number one is I acknowledge the objection. Number two is I isolate the objection. And number three is I tie them down and I get them to commit. ## [42:17 - 45:17]

So what I do is every time, every single part of the conversation, I always have a follow up process, right? So the idea is this. The idea is if you have to control every part of the buying journey. So if there's multiple steps in that sales process, you need a beginning step and an end step on how to control, how to hold their hand throughout the entire process. You wanna hold their hand until you walk them to the finish line. Because at any point during that sales process, if you let go of their hands, they get lost in the woods. You have to do the whole bullshit all over again. Don't do that. Control it from beginning to end. So the way that you control every time you have a conversation and when you're gonna get off the phone and follow it up with the next call, number one is you define next steps. And while you're on the call or on the video, you have them accepted if they can't, they might be walking whatever. If they can accept it, have them accepted. just keeps on making it grow bigger and bigger and bigger and creates a better experience for the customer. So the sales flywheel is very simple.
